coredata.dc:title: Mechanistic insights into tunable luminescence and persistent luminescence of the full-color-emitting BCNO phosphors
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lei
Zhang, Jianhua
Qu, Bingyan
Wu, Qisheng
Zhou, Rulong
Li, Dongdong
Zhang, Bo
Ren, Mingxi
Zeng, Xiao Cheng
coredata.dc:description: Boron carbon oxynitride (BCNO) phosphors can exhibit remarkable tunable emission colors and greenish persistent luminescence without the assistant of rare-earth ion dopants. However, the photoluminescent and persistent luminescence (PL) mechanisms of BCNO are still not fully understood. In this work, we attempt to reveal distinct photoluminescent and PL mechanism of BCNO based on the first-principles computation. Our results show that the tunable emission of BCNO stems from the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in the host BN lattice rather than originally expected from isolated carbon impurity. The embedded CQDs can emit light with size-dependent wavelength (color). Furthermore, oxygen ions chemisorbed at the edge of BCNO can passivate the dangling bonds, and thereby enhance BCNO's quantum efficiency. The PL mechanism can thus be attributed to the defect levels within the band gap, induced by CQDs. This mechanism is distinctly different from the conventional PL mechanism for the rare-earth-metal doped aluminate phosphors [Chem. Mater. 2015, 27, 2195–2202]. The obtained new insights into the luminescent and PL mechanisms of BCNO will not only benefit engineering novel optical materials with improved PL properties, but also promote future R&D efforts in exploiting broader application opportunities for the BCNO family phosphors.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Atomic and electronic structures of C-related defects
Atomic and electronic structures of O-related defects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No: 51302059, 11404085 and 51601051), NSAF (Grant No: U1630118), China Scholarship Council (201606090079 and 201606695027), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (Grant 1708085ME121) and Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of Hefei University of Technology (2016CXCYS019). X.C.Z. is supported by a Qian-ren B (One Thousand Talent Plan B) summer research fund from USTC, and by a State Key R&D Fund of China (2016YFA0200604) to USTC. Calculations were done in the Supercomputing Center of University of Science and Technology of China and Holland Supercomputing Center in University of Nebraska-Lincoln.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The supercell of BN. Color scheme: green is for B and grey for N. (b) The computed band structure and density of the states (DOS) of a perfect BN sheet. The Fermi level is set to be 0 eV. The inset shows a sketch of the first Brillouin zone of the BN monolayer. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Computed band structure and density of the state of (a) CB and (b) CN. The Fermi level is set to be 0 eV. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Various structure models for the C-related complex defects in BN. Color scheme: green for B, grey for N and brown for C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Computed electronic band dispersion, DOS and PDOS of the C2 defect. The Fermi level is set to be 0 eV. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Computed electronic band dispersion, DOS and PDOS due to the complex defect: (a) C6, (b) C24, (c) C54 and (d) C96. The Fermi level is set to be 0 eV. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Computed imaginary part of the dielectric function of BN with different CQDs. The inset is in an enlarged view of dielectric function. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Computed DOS of C24 + C54 + C96 model system. (b) A comparison of the PDOS of isolated C24, C54 and C96 in Fig. 3(f) (red lines) and in Fig. 3(c–e) (blue lines), respectively. (c) The imaginary part of the dielectric function of the C24 + C54 + C96 model system. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The computed first peak of the imaginary part of the dielectric function vs the size of the CQDs. The red and black line represent the results at HSE06 and PBE level. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The structure models and the band structures of the C24 embedded BN with (a) the substitution doping of one O atom, and (b) the chemisorption of one O atom on the surface. The Fermi level is set to be 0 eV. For O chemisorption, both top view and side view are shown. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Two structural models and computed band structures of BN with dangling bonds (a) and without dangling bonds (b). Color scheme: green is for B, grey for N, red for O and pink for H. The Fermi level is set to be 0 eV. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Mechanistic insights into tunable luminescence and persistent luminescence of the full-color-emitting BCNO phosphors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry and Nebraska Center for Materials and Nanoscience, University of Nebraska-Lincoln, Lincoln, NE 68588, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Nebraska Center for Materials and Nanoscience

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron carbon oxynitride (BCNO) phosphors can exhibit remarkable tunable emission colors and greenish persistent luminescence without the assistant of rare-earth ion dopants. However, the photoluminescent and persistent luminescence (PL) mechanisms of BCNO are still not fully understood. In this work, we attempt to reveal distinct photoluminescent and PL mechanism of BCNO based on the first-principles computation. Our results show that the tunable emission of BCNO stems from the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in the host BN lattice rather than originally expected from isolated carbon impurity. The embedded CQDs can emit light with size-dependent wavelength (color). Furthermore, oxygen ions chemisorbed at the edge of BCNO can passivate the dangling bonds, and thereby enhance BCNO's quantum efficiency. The PL mechanism can thus be attributed to the defect levels within the band gap, induced by CQDs. This mechanism is distinctly different from the conventional PL mechanism for the rare-earth-metal doped aluminate phosphors [Chem. Mater. 2015, 27, 2195–2202]. The obtained new insights into the luminescent and PL mechanisms of BCNO will not only benefit engineering novel optical materials with improved PL properties, but also promote future R&D efforts in exploiting broader application opportunities for the BCNO family phosphors.

ce:section.ce:para: Boron carbon oxynitrides (BCNO), a new class of rare-earth-element-free phosphors, have attracted considerable attention due to their unique luminescent properties, such as high quantum efficiency (the optimum external quantum efficiencies amount to as high as 79%) and tunable emission wavelength across the whole spectrum of visible light via adjusting carbon concentration [1,2] or preparation conditions [3]. Some BCNO phosphors also exhibit amazing persistent luminescence (PL) in green-light region with decay time of about 2 h [4], comparable to rare-earth-element doped aluminates phosphors or silicates phosphors. These fascinating properties endow BCNO with wide potential for applications in light emitting diodes (LEDs) [5–7], white light emission [8], bio-imaging probe [9], DNA labelling [10], dosimeter for gamma doses, and even sensors for Cu2+ determination in water [11]. However, the fundamental mechanism underlying the photoluminescence and PL of BCNO is still not fully clear, although there have been many attempts to put forward one. Liu et al. [4] assumed that the light emission was originated from the electron transition between impurity levels of carbon doped in the hexagonal compound of BNO, rather than the inter-band transition as had been observed with bulk BN crystals or BN nanotubes [10]. Wang et al. [1] attributed the emission of BCNO phosphors to the closed-shell BO− and BO2− anions. Zhang et al. [12] suggested that the emission of BCNO stems from the electron transition from the nitrogen vacancies levels to the impurity levels of carbon whose distribution can be broadened by increasing carbon concentration. Hence, the emission spectra can be tunable. Kang et al. [13] found that the luminescence can be derived from intrinsic state emission (electron-hole recombination) and defect state emission (surface energy traps) in BCNO. Also, Liu et al. [4] attributed the PL to the thermally assisted tunneling recombination of electrons, released from the trap centers of N vacancies with the empty C-related impurity level. However, little evidence for this explanation is found. So, a widely accepted explanation about the photoluminescent mechanism of BCNO is greatly needed in general, while identification of the main composition and local structure responsible for emitting the tunable light is needed in particular.

One possible reason for the apparent disagreement among many different versions of the photoluminescent mechanism is due to the uncertainty on the exact atomic structures and electronic structures of the BCNO. Previous experiments indicated that B and N crystallize into turbostratic boron nitride (t-BN) structure [2,4,14], while C and O might exist in t-BN as dopants. Since the concentration of C and O is quite high [15], they may form certain local structures in BN. The facts that the emission wavelength can be tuned by different C concentration, and that the quantum efficiency of BCNO is much higher than that of BCN (with quantum efficiency being only about 1%) [16] imply that the C- and O-related local structures may play a significant role in the luminescence of BCNO. The distribution and local structures of the C-related defects are expected to change with the increase of C concentration. Most experiments can only provide information about the chemical bonds presented in the BCNO compound, but not the exact arrangements of C and O atoms. For example, BO, CN, CC and CO bonds were detected in many experiments while the observation of BC bonds was rare [17–19]. A lack of detailed atomic structures of C- and O-related defects hinders exploration of the luminescent mechanism and thus wide applications of the BCNO.

In this work, we provide an account of the luminescent mechanism of the BCNO, based on comprehensive first-principles computations. Our computational results indicate that the C impurities in t-BN favor aggregation into C quantum dots (CQDs). These CQDs, when embedded in BN, can induce suitable defect levels whose distributions are dependent on the sizes of CQDs. Electron transition among these defect levels of the CQDs can lead to light emission with wavelength ranging from blue to red color. So the tunable light emission of the BCNO mainly stems from the CQDs themselves. On the other hand, the PL of the BCNO can be attributed to numerous defect levels induced by CQDs, where the excited electrons and holes can experience thermal relaxation processes, thereby prolonging the PL decay time. Moreover, the O atoms tend to gather at the boundary between the CQDs and BN, rendering the light emission of CQDs red-shifted. The O atoms can also enhance the quantum efficiency of the BCNO by saturating the dangling bonds at the edge of BN sheets. These new insights into the luminescent mechanism are consistent with the previous experimental observations, thereby confirm the rationality of the identified mechanisms.

The supercell for the system contains 8 × 8 × 1 primitive monolayer BN unit cells (see Fig. 1 (a)). A vacuum spacing of 10 Å was chosen to ensure that the interaction between the layers in adjacent supercells can be neglected. The calculations were performed using the projector augmented wave (PAW) pseudopotentials as implemented in the VASP 5.4 code [20,21]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerof (PBE) [22] exchange-correlation functional within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was selected. The electronic wave functions were described by a set of plane-wave basis with the energy cutoff of 400 eV. The Brillouin-zone integrations were calculated by using the Monkhorst-Pack grids [23], for which a 2 × 2 × 1 Γ-centered k-point mesh was adopted.

The obtained results are presented in two parts. In the first part, the atomic and electronic structures of C-related defects in the BN sheet are discussed, from which the mechanism of photoluminescence and PL is illustrated. In the second part, the results about the O-related defects in the CQD embedded BN sheets are discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic and electronic structures of C-related defects
ce:section.ce:para: First, we study C-doped BN monolayer to examine the effect of C. The electronic structure of a prefect BN monolayer with an 8 × 8 supercell is as given in Fig. 1, showing a band gap of 4.57 eV, about 1.40 eV lower than the experimental value (5.97 eV) [24]. From the computed total and partial density of the states (DOS and PDOS) shown in Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that the higher valence bands and lower conduction bands are mainly contributed by N 2p and B 2p states, respectively.

The electronic structures of the C-doped BN with the carbon substitution at B (denoted as CB) and N (denoted as CN) sites are shown in Fig. 2 . Clearly, the doping of CB introduces an electron occupied spin-up and an empty spin-down defect energy levels, which are located at 1.26 eV and 0.35 eV, respectively, below the conduction band minimum (CBM). The doping of CN also introduces an electron occupied spin-up and an empty spin-down defect energy levels which are located at 0.29 eV and 1.22 eV above the valence band maximum (VBM). For single-atom doping per supercell, the electrons can only transit between the defect levels and the conduction or valence bands of BN, which cannot emit visible light due to large energy differences among these levels. Hence, the observed tunable emission of visible light should not be due to the singly doped C.

Experiments have shown that the light emission color of BCNO can be tuned by C concentration. So we need to consider more C atoms doping per supercell into the BN sheet. Because the C impurities in BN favor to bond with one another, C clusters (also named as C quantum dots (CQD) in this study) may form when more C atoms are doped into the BN sheet. In realistic samples, different sizes of CQDs may coexist. To examine the dependence of the electronic structures of C-doped BN on the size of the CQDs, we consider four different sizes of CQDs in the same BN supercell, denoted as C6, C24, C54 and C96 respectively. Their atomic structures and corresponding electronic structures are shown in Figs. 3(b–e) and 5(a–d) , respectively. Fig. 5 shows that each CQD induces several defect energy levels within the band gap of the BN sheet. These defect levels can be divided into two groups according to their position with respect to the Fermi level. The defect levels below the Fermi level are fully occupied by electrons while those above the Fermi level are empty. The gap between the highest occupied and the lowest empty defect levels of CQDs would dictate the color of emission light. With increasing the size of the CQDs, the highest occupied and lowest empty defect levels move up and down, respectively, resulting in smaller energy difference between the two defect levels. The energy difference between the occupied and empty defect levels is 3.57 eV for C6, 2.32 eV for C24, 1.54 eV for C54 and 1.01 eV for C96, respectively, an energy range covering the light wavelength from ultraviolet to infrared.

To further identify that the electrons can transit between both defect levels of CQDs, we compute the imaginary part of the dielectric function (in proportion to the absorption of light) due to these defects, and that of the perfect and C2-doped BN sheet, all shown in Fig. 6 . For the perfect BN, the absorption edge is at about 4.60 eV (the black line in Fig. 6), consistent with the computed band gap of 4.57 eV. With the C2 and C6 defects, their first absorption peaks are at about 3.60 eV, which are clearly related to the electron transition between the defect levels induced by the C atoms. When the size of the C defect clusters grows, the first absorption peak shifts towards lower energy significantly, and its energy matches the energy difference between the occupied and empty defect levels of the corresponding CQD. Hence, the electron transition between the defect levels is permitted. For realistic samples where different sizes of CQDs can coexist, light emission with different colors may be observed when the samples are excited.

Also for realistic samples with relatively high C concentration and coexistence of different sized CQDs, it is important to take into account the interactions among these CQDs on the luminescent properties of BCNO. To this end, we construct a larger supercell, which contains 16 × 16 × 1 primitive monolayer BN unit cells. Three different sized CQDs (C24, C54 and C96) are embedded into the supercell all together (see Fig. 3(f)), named as C24 + C54 + C96. As shown in Fig. 3(f), the distances between the separate CQDs are about 8 Å, which may result in weak interaction among them. The computed density of states (Fig. 7 (a)) indicate that many defect levels can be induced within the band gap, mostly coming from the C 2p states. The energy difference between the highest occupied and the lowest unoccupied defect levels is very close to that due to the single C96 embedded in BN sheet (see Fig. 5(d)). Fig. 7(b) shows a comparison of the PDOS of each CQD in the combined C24 + C54 + C96 (red line) with that of the single CQD embedded BN sheet (blue line). It can be seen that the defect levels of each CQD in both environments almost coincide with each other. This result suggests that the interactions among different CQDs in the BN sheet have weak influence on the electronic structures and luminescent property of each CQD. This conclusion is further confirmed by the calculated imaginary part of dielectric function as shown in Fig. 7(c). Three different absorption peaks with the corresponding energy value within the visible light range can be seen, corresponding to the absorption peaks of C96, C54 and C24, respectively. The shape and location of these peaks are also similar to those due to the isolated C96, C54 and C24 CQDs (Fig. 6).

Since the emission wavelength depends on the size of CQDs embedded in the BN sheet, it is important to know approximate emission wavelength for a given CQD. To this end, we plot the position of the first absorption peak for CQDs with various sizes (Fig. 8 ). Since the PBE functional tends to underestimate the band gap of insulators and semiconductors, we also compute the band structures of different CQD embedded BN sheet and that of a perfect BN sheet, using the Heyd-Scuseria-Ernzerhof (HSE06) hybrid functional [28,29], to obtain more accurate band gap. The HSE06 band gap of the BN sheet is about 5.47 eV, much closer to the experimental value of 5.97 eV [24], suggesting that the HSE06 functional is likely to give reasonable prediction of energy difference between the highest occupied and lowest empty energy levels. Based on the computed energy differences from the PBE and HSE06 computation, we provide an estimation of the position of the first absorption peak of each CQD. As shown in Fig. 8, the energy value of the first absorption peak decreases significantly with increasing the size of the CQDs. Specially, when the size is 7.5 Å, 12.4 Å and 17.3 Å, the first absorption peak is located at about 2.98 eV, 2.15 eV and 1.78 eV (HSE06 level), respectively, corresponding to blue, green and red light. This trend of the position of the first absorption peak is very similar to the case of the free CQDs (which is not embedded in any host lattice; see Ref. [30]), although the energy differences between the highest occupied level and the lowest unoccupied level in the free CQDs are much larger (7.3 eV, 4.2 eV, 3.0 eV and 2.2 eV for the free C6, C24, C54 and C96, respectively). Obviously, the host lattice BN can lower the energy difference between the occupied and unoccupied defect levels significantly. From the trend of the position of the first absorption peak, one expects that when the size of the CQDs increases further, the CQDs would emit infrared light, which can be very useful for application in bio-nanotechnology [31].

Based on the results discussed above, we can offer an explanation of the tunable photoluminescent mechanism of the BCNO sample. The doped C atoms in the BN sheet tend to segregate and form different sized CQDs. Those different-sized CQDs can emit light with different color so that the light emitted by a realistic BCNO sample is a sum of the emission due to these CQDs. When the C concentration is low, only small sized CQDs dominate the emission, and the light with relatively short wavelengths can be emitted. As the C concentration increases, larger sized CQDs arise so that the light emission is red-shifted due to the smaller energy difference between the occupied and empty defect levels. Also, in high C concentration condition, various sizes of CQDs can coexist in the BN sheet, light with different color should be detected, a notion consistent with the experimental observation that broad emission spectra with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) can amount to about 150 nm (corresponding to ∼0.8 eV) [2].

Another evidence for this explanation is the red-shifted emission with the increase of the wavelength of the light for excitation [14]. When the BCNO samples are excited by a high-energy UV light, both small- and large-sized CQDs can be excited. On the contrary, when the samples are excited by a light with a longer wavelength, the small-sized CQDs cannot be excited due to their large energy gap between the occupied and empty defect levels. Hence, only the large-sized CQDs can be excited. Hence, as the wavelength of the excitation light increases, the emission light tends to shift towards longer wavelength.

Previous experiments also show that the BCNO sample possesses persistent luminescence in green color, while the persistent luminescence in blue or red color is relatively weaker. Based on the electronic structures, we can also explain this phenomenon. According to Fig. 8, the CQDs with the size of about C54 may emit green light. In Fig. 5(c) we can see that the empty and occupied defect levels introduced by the C54 distribute densely below the conduction bands and above the valence bands of the BN sheet, respectively, with the energy difference between neighboring levels less than 0.5 eV. Such a dense distribution of empty and occupied defect levels of CQDs ensures that the electron carriers can transit between the empty defect levels of the CQDs and the conduction bands of the BN sheet, and the hole carriers can transit between the occupied defect levels of the CQDs and the valence bands of the BN sheet, respectively. When the sample is excited by high-energy light, the electrons in the occupied defect levels of the CQD are excited into the unoccupied defect levels. The excited electrons should transit back to its ground state so that visible light is emitted. However, because there are many close defect levels below the CBM and above the VBM, the excited electrons can easily transit into higher defect levels and even the conduction band. And also, the electrons in the valence bands can also transit into the empty defect levels just above the VBM, leaving hole carriers in the valence bands. Before recombination, electrons and holes can experience complex processes such as thermal vibration and phonon coupling so that they can move up and down among those densely distributed defect levels of the CQDs, and among the conduction and valence bands at room temperature. These relaxation processes can greatly enhance the life time of both electron and hole carriers, and subsequently prolong the green PL decay time.

For the CQD with smaller size, as shown in Fig. 5(b), the energy differences between the neighboring defect levels are larger than those shown in Fig. 5(c), and so it is more difficult for the excited electron to transfer between these defect levels. Hence, the blue PL should be weaker. For the CQD with larger size, as shown in Fig. 5(d), the distribution of the defect levels within the band gap becomes even denser. However, we note that the distance between the highest occupied defect levels and the VBM and that between the lowest unoccupied defect levels and the CBM become wider. So, the excited electrons (holes) have to go through a more complex process and a longer time to reach the lowest unoccupied defect levels (the highest occupied defect levels). Hence, these electrons (holes) may have a much higher possibility to transfer back to the ground state through nonradiative transition. This may be the reason why the red PL reported in the literature is also weaker than the green PL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic and electronic structures of O-related defects
ce:section.ce:para: From the discussions above, we predict that the CQDs can be incorporated into the BN compound and the emission of the BCNO mostly comes from the electron transition between the defect levels induced by CQDs. However, the role of O played in the photoluminescence of the BCNO is still unclear. Here, the influence of O on the luminescent properties of BCNO is investigated. For simplicity, the C24 embedded structure (shown in Fig. 3(c)) is taken as an example. Due to the lack of the structural information of O in BCNO, we consider all the possible O-related defects which can be divided into three types. The first type is substitution of O for N or C. Since NO bond has rarely been observed in experiments, the substitution of O for B is not considered here. The second type is the chemisorption of O atom at the surface of BCN, and the third one is the chemisorption of O atom at the edge of BN.

For the substitution doping of one O atom in the BN with C24 embedded in, various doping sites are considered. Detailed structures and stabilities of them are discussed in the Supporting Information. Among them, the most energetically favorite site is the N site which is very close to the boundary between the CQD and BN while not bonding directly with the CQD, as shown in Fig. 9 (a). Its electronic structure shows that the O atom only introduces deep defect levels in the valence bands but has little influence on the energy levels within the band gap, which are originated from the CQD. So the substitution doping of O would have little influence on the emission of CQDs.

Next, we study the chemisorption of O on the surface of the BCN sheet. Similarly, various adsorption sites on the BCN sheet have been considered (details given in Supporting Information). The most favorite adsorption site for O is above the center of the BC bond at the boundary between the CQD and BN (Fig. 9(b)). Because of the adsorption of O, the BC bond is broken. Different from the substitution, the adsorption of O have some influence on the band-gap states of CQD (Fig. 9(b)), which is surely due to the formation of the CO bond. The energy difference between the highest occupied and lowest empty energy levels of CQD is narrowed from 2.32 eV to 2.17 eV by the chemisorption of an O atom.

We further considered more O atoms chemisorbed above the other BC bonds in the boundary between the CQD and BN, up to six O atoms. Details are discussed in the Supporting Information. For the adsorption of different number of O atoms, all have slight influence on the distribution of the defect energy levels of the CQD, while not introducing any defect levels in the band gap directly (Fig. S7). The energy difference between the occupied and empty levels of CQD decreases to about 2.0 eV when two to five O atoms chemisorbed on the sheet because of the splitting of both the top occupied levels and bottom empty levels of the CQD. When six O atoms are chemisorbed on the BCN sheet, due to the symmetrized distribution of O atoms, both the top occupied levels and bottom empty levels of the CQD become degenerated again. Hence, the energy difference between them increases to 2.25 eV. These results indicate that the chemisorbed O atoms do not participate in the luminescent process of CQDs directly, but can make the emission of CQDs red-shifted through decreasing the energy difference between the occupied and empty defect levels of CQDs.

In realistic samples, BN is typically surrounded by edges where large amount of dangling bonds may exist. O atoms can be chemisorbed at the edge of the BN sheets, lowering the number of dangling bonds. For simplicity, we construct a model with edges in the center of the BN supercell, as shown in Fig. 10 (a). The computed band structure (shown in Fig. 10(a)) shows that a large number of defect levels densely distribute over a wide energy range within the band gap. The PDOS of these defect levels suggest that the levels near VBM is mainly contributed by the 2p states of N at the edge, while the levels near CBM mostly come from the 2p states of B at the edge. These defect levels may have negative influence on the emission of the BCNO, since the electrons excited in BCNO can enter these defect levels through nonradiative relaxation. So in a O-free BCN system, although light emission occurs, the efficiency is low, as observed in experiment [16].

For the BCNO system, the light emission efficiency is much higher than the BCN system, implying that the doping of O can eliminate the dangling bonds in the edge of BN. In the experiments [17], besides the BO, CO bonds, OH and NH bonds are also observed. So the dangling bonds at the edge of the BN sheet may be saturated by the adsorption of H at N atom and OH group at B atom respectively. Hence, we construct a structural model as shown in Fig. 10(b) where six H atoms and six OH groups are bonded to the N atoms and B atoms at the edge, respectively. The computed electronic structure shows that almost all the defect levels induced by the dangling bonds disappear. So the nonradiative relaxation of excited electrons to the dangling bonds at the edges can be cut off. This may be the main reason why O dopants can enhance the light emission efficiency of BCNO.

In this work, we have investigated the atomic structures and electronic structures of C- and O-related defects, from which the photoluminescence and PL mechanism of the BCNO are derived. The main results can be summarized as following: (1) C atoms in BN monolayer prefer to be close together, and as the resulting CQDs are formed; (2) The emission of the BCNO may stem from the electron transition between the defect levels of CQDs embedded in BN, and the emission of CQDs is size-dependent; (3) The PL of BCNO may be caused by a large number of defect levels of CQDs, which are densely distributed over the energy range below the CBM and above the VBM; (4) The O atom can be chemisorbed at the boundary between the CQD and BN, resulting the emission of the CQDs is red-shifted; (5) The O atom can eliminate the dangling bonds at the edge of BN, and enhance the quantum efficiency of the emission. Our results provide a deeper understanding of the photoluminescent and the PL nature of the BCNO, which will be very helpful to engineering the luminescent properties and to offering broader application of this type of phosphors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No: 51302059, 11404085 and 51601051), NSAF (Grant No: U1630118), China Scholarship Council (201606090079 and 201606695027), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (Grant 1708085ME121) and Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of Hefei University of Technology (2016CXCYS019). X.C.Z. is supported by a Qian-ren B (One Thousand Talent Plan B) summer research fund from USTC, and by a State Key R&D Fund of China (2016YFA0200604) to USTC. Calculations were done in the Supercomputing Center of University of Science and Technology of China and Holland Supercomputing Center in University of Nebraska-Lincoln.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.06.054.
